characters.relation: Nymph counselor and reputed consort
characters.blurb: Divine muse who met Numa in the sacred grove and taught him rites. Skeptics said he invented her to awe the people.

characters.relation: Sabine co-king under Romulus
characters.blurb: Former Sabine king whose people Numa came from. His murder left Rome ready for a peaceful successor to Romulus.

characters.relation: First wife; daughter of Tatius
characters.blurb: Sabine wife who died young; through her Numa was tied to Tatius’s house before he took Rome’s throne.

characters.relation: Daughter; mother of Ancus’s line
characters.blurb: Numa’s daughter, married (in one account) to Marcius; grandmother of Ancus Marcius, Rome’s fourth king.

characters.relation: Son-in-law; father of Ancus
characters.blurb: Rival candidate when Numa died; father of Ancus Marcius. His line kept Sabine blood on the throne.

characters.relation: Grandson; later king
characters.blurb: Ancus Marcius, fourth king of Rome, sprung from Pompilia—Numa’s peaceful blood continued after Tullus’s wars.

characters.relation: Warlike successor
characters.blurb: Third king who scorned Numa’s quiet rites, filled Rome with war, and finally tried to imitate Numa’s Jupiter cult—badly.

characters.relation: Predecessor; warlike founder
characters.blurb: First king whose arms left Rome fierce. The people chose Numa to temper that spirit with religion and law.

characters.relation: Deified Romulus
characters.blurb: The god Romulus became; Numa gave him priests and a quieter civic religion beside Jupiter and Mars.

characters.relation: Sage some linked to Numa
characters.blurb: Greek philosopher whose doctrines of silence, numbers, and abstinence later writers foisted onto Numa—Plutarch weighs the chronology carefully.

characters.relation: School compared to Numa’s discipline
characters.blurb: Followers whose shared meals, music, and sacred rules reminded Romans of Numa’s priesthoods—whether or not he met their master.

characters.relation: Chief god of his sky-rites
characters.blurb: Numa bargained with Jupiter over thunderbolt expiations and kept Terminus and Fides in the Capitol’s settlement of gods.

characters.relation: War-god given Salii and peace-bound spears
characters.blurb: Numa instituted the Salii for Mars and taught that the god’s spears should rest while justice held—war as last resort.

characters.relation: Hearth goddess; Vestal order
characters.blurb: Numa founded (or ordered) the Vestal Virgins and the undying fire—the city’s sacred hearth.

characters.relation: Dancing priests of Mars
characters.blurb: Twelve priests who carried the ancilia and leapt in armor; Numa founded their college after the shield fell from heaven.

characters.relation: Heaven-sent shield
characters.blurb: Shield said to fall from the sky as a pledge of empire. Numa had eleven copies made so thieves could not steal the true one.

characters.relation: Smith of the ancilia copies
characters.blurb: Craftsman who forged eleven duplicate shields; rewarded by having his name sung in the Salian hymn.

characters.relation: God of boundaries
characters.blurb: Boundary-stone god who refused to yield even to Jupiter on the Capitol—emblem of Numa’s settled property and peace.

characters.relation: Goddesses of his grove
characters.blurb: Numa’s sacred spring and grove belonged to the Camenae/Muses; Egeria was their chief counselor in the legend.

characters.relation: Early Italian prophetic figure
characters.blurb: Mythic king or woodpecker-spirit named among Italy’s oldest religious lore that Numa’s age still remembered.

characters.relation: Rustic prophetic god
characters.blurb: Italian woodland god paired with Picus in tales of early Latium’s oracles and rites.

characters.relation: Philosopher cited on kingship
characters.blurb: Invoked when Plutarch compares Numa’s philosopher-king ideal to Greek schemes of rule.

characters.relation: Early Roman annalist cited
characters.blurb: Among the Roman authorities Plutarch consults for Numa’s chronology and institutions.

characters.relation: Annalist cited
characters.blurb: Roman historian whose numbers and marvels Plutarch sometimes repeats with caution.

characters.relation: Later sackers who tested his peace
characters.blurb: Their capture of Rome generations later frames how fragile Numa’s unarmed quiet proved without martial heirs.

characters.relation: Greek pair in the Parallel Lives
characters.blurb: Spartan lawgiver Plutarch sets beside Numa—both civilizers, one by austerity, one by rites.

characters.relation: King and lawgiver; subject of the Life
characters.blurb: Sabine philosopher-king who tempered Rome’s martial spirit with religion, establishing priests, Vestals, and a peace-minded calendar after Romulus.

characters.relation: Witness of Romulus’s apotheosis; Numa’s ambassador
characters.blurb: Swore to the Romans that Romulus had ascended as Quirinus; later led the embassy that offered Numa the throne alongside the Sabine Velesus.

characters.relation: Sabine rival candidate for the kingship
characters.blurb: Sabine nobleman named alongside Proculus as the man the Sabine faction preferred to succeed Romulus; the Roman party preferred Proculus.

characters.relation: Goddess of April in Numa’s calendar
characters.blurb: Numa’s reformed calendar named April from Venus (or Aphrodite); the women bathed in myrtle garlands on the first day in her honor.

characters.relation: God whose Greek name gave Rome a priestly title
characters.blurb: The Latin word for a temple attendant (flamen) was traced by some to the Greek name for Mercury, showing Numa’s institutions drew on Greek religious vocabulary.

characters.relation: Son of Numa; eponym of the Aemilii
characters.blurb: One of Numa’s four sons, named Mamercus after a son of Pythagoras; the noble house of Aemilii claimed descent from him and that Pythagorean connection.

characters.relation: Sicyonian hero favored by Apollo; parallel to Numa
characters.blurb: A Sicyonian said to be so beloved by Apollo that the god would utter an oracular verse whenever he put to sea—one of the divine-intimacy parallels Plutarch cites near Egeria.

characters.relation: Later emperor; renamed a month in Numa’s calendar
characters.blurb: Caesar Augustus renamed the month Sextilis after himself, as Julius Caesar had named July; Plutarch notes both in reviewing Numa’s original month-count.

characters.relation: Cross-reference to the Life of Camillus
characters.blurb: Plutarch directs readers to Camillus’s Life for the full account of the Palladium’s rites—mysteries Numa is said to have established.

characters.relation: Learned king who explained Roman words
characters.blurb: Cited twice for etymologies of Roman priestly vocabulary and the robe called Laena—a polymath king eager to trace Roman institutions back to Greek origins.

characters.relation: Eponymous ancestor of the house of Aemilii
characters.blurb: Numa gave his son Mamercus the surname Aemilius for his graceful speech; that son became the eponym of the ancient patrician family.

characters.relation: God of beginnings; gave January its name
characters.blurb: Numa placed January before March in the calendar, naming it from Janus and giving precedence to the god of peaceful beginnings over Mars the war-god.

characters.relation: Annalist who questioned Rome's early records
characters.blurb: Wrote Strictures on Chronology, arguing that Rome's ancient registers were destroyed in the Gallic sack and that the extant ones were forgeries designed to flatter noble families—making all early chronology of Numa suspect.

characters.relation: Published the Olympic victor lists used for dating
characters.blurb: Hippias of Elis, sophist and polymath, who at a late date compiled the first systematic list of Olympic victors—the tool chronologers used to date Lycurgus and Numa, though Plutarch notes it rests on no firm authority.

characters.relation: Numa's father
characters.blurb: Illustrious Sabine of Cures described simply as 'an illustrious person'; Numa was the youngest of his four sons, born—so the tradition insisted—on the very day Rome was founded.

characters.relation: Cretan king-lawgiver who claimed divine guidance
characters.blurb: Legendary king of Crete who received his laws from Zeus; cited alongside Zaleucus, Zoroaster, Lycurgus, and Numa as rulers who invoked divine authority to make new institutions acceptable to their peoples.

characters.relation: Locrian lawgiver who used divine authority
characters.blurb: Lawgiver of the Western Locrians who claimed the goddess Athena had dictated his code—cited by Plutarch alongside Minos, Zoroaster, and Lycurgus as examples of legislators who found divine sanction expedient.

characters.relation: Persian sage-lawgiver cited as parallel to Numa
characters.blurb: Founder of Zoroastrianism cited by Plutarch among the great lawgivers—Minos, Zaleucus, Lycurgus, Numa—who claimed divine inspiration to give their innovations the force of heaven-sent authority.

characters.relation: Roman annalist; source on Numa's death
characters.blurb: Lucius Calpurnius Piso Frugi, early Roman annalist; Plutarch cites him specifically for the fact that Numa died not of any sudden disease but of a peaceful old age with a gradual decline.

characters.relation: Numa's son; eponym of the Pomponii
characters.blurb: One of the four sons attributed to Numa in the fuller tradition; from him descended the noble family of the Pomponii—a genealogy some ancient writers suspected of being invented to flatter that house.

characters.relation: Numa's son; eponym of the Pinarii
characters.blurb: One of the four sons in the extended tradition; from him descended the ancient patrician Pinarii—a family whose descent from Numa, like the others, was questioned by skeptical historians.

characters.relation: Numa's son; eponym of the Calpurnii
characters.blurb: One of the four sons named in the fuller genealogy; ancestor of the Calpurnii—another noble house that traced its dignity back to the philosopher-king's line.

characters.relation: Alternative second wife of Numa
characters.blurb: Named in one tradition as another wife whom Numa married after becoming king—the source of Pompilia according to writers who doubted she was Tatia's daughter. Distinct from the more famous Lucretia of the Tarquin tale.

characters.relation: Emperor who renamed two months
characters.blurb: Emperor who, imitating Julius Caesar and Augustus, renamed September and October 'Germanicus' and 'Domitianus'; on his assassination both months reverted—mentioned when Plutarch traces how Numa's original calendar was overlaid by later names.

characters.relation: Consul when Janus's gates were shut in peace
characters.blurb: Consul (with Titus Manlius) in one of the vanishingly rare years when the gates of Janus's temple were closed, signifying universal peace—cited to show how unusual Numa's forty-three years of peace really were.

characters.relation: Co-consul with Atilius when Janus closed
characters.blurb: Consul alongside Marcus Atilius in the one pre-Augustan year when the gates of Janus were shut in peacetime—the unique historical moment Plutarch names to measure how extraordinary Numa's unbroken peace of forty-three years truly was.

characters.relation: Praetor who ordered Numa's books burnt
characters.blurb: Roman praetor who, when Numa's buried books were unearthed centuries later, read them and swore before the senate that their contents were unfit to be made public—the volumes were then carried to the Comitium and burned.

characters.relation: Consul when Numa's buried books were found
characters.blurb: P. Cornelius, one of the two consuls in office when heavy rains washed away the hillside at the Janiculum and exposed the stone chests containing Numa's body and books—the strange antiquarian episode reported by Valerius Antias.

characters.relation: Consul alongside Cornelius when books emerged
characters.blurb: M. Baebius, the other consul present when the torrential rains of their year uncovered Numa's buried coffins—one found empty of any body, the other full of the sacred books that Petilius then ordered destroyed.

characters.relation: Defeated by Augustus, enabling a rare peace
characters.blurb: Mark Antony, the triumvir whose defeat by Augustus brought such complete peace that the gates of Janus could be shut for the first time since Numa—one of only two such closures in all Roman history before Augustus.

characters.relation: Caesar's defeated rival; his defeat renamed July
characters.blurb: Roman general whose defeat by Julius Caesar led to the renaming of the month Quintilis as July in Caesar's honor—the first of the month-name changes that Plutarch reviews when tracing what Numa's original calendar became.

characters.relation: Sceptical philosopher; wrote on Pythagoras
characters.blurb: Phliasian philosopher and sceptic who composed a satirical distich mocking Pythagoras for his miraculous pretensions; Plutarch cites it when listing the divine-intimacy claims surrounding Pythagoras that later writers attached to Numa.

characters.relation: Iambic poet honored by the gods after death
characters.blurb: Paros-born archaic poet; Plutarch cites the tradition that the Muses honored Hesiod and Archilochus after their deaths to argue that if gods visit poets in sport, they might well guide lawgivers in earnest.

characters.relation: Archaic poet honored by the gods; parallel to Numa
characters.blurb: Boeotian poet of Works and Days; the tradition that the gods rendered him honor after death is cited by Plutarch to support the plausibility of Numa's divine conversations with Egeria.

characters.relation: Lyric poet beloved of Pan; parallel to Numa
characters.blurb: Theban lyric poet; Pan is reported to have fallen in love with Pindar for his verses—a story Plutarch uses to show that divine attachment to human beings of genius was not implausible and so to support the Egeria tradition.

characters.relation: Lyric poet quoted on tolerance of disagreement
characters.blurb: Cean lyric poet; quoted for the phrase 'the road is broad'—used by Plutarch to say there is room for differing opinions about whether gods truly visited lawgivers like Lycurgus and Numa.

characters.relation: Comic writer who recorded Pythagoras as Roman citizen
characters.blurb: Sicilian comic playwright and Pythagorean; in a book dedicated to Antenor he recorded that Pythagoras had been made a Roman citizen—one of the external proofs Plutarch marshals for a connection between Pythagoras and Numa.

characters.relation: Dedicatee of Epicharmus's book on Pythagoras
characters.blurb: The person to whom Epicharmus dedicated his work recording Pythagoras's honorary Roman citizenship—otherwise unknown, but his name survives as the dedicatee that preserves a key piece of Pythagorean–Roman tradition.

characters.relation: Athenian tyrant under whom the sacred fire went out
characters.blurb: Athenian who briefly held tyrannical power; during his tenure the perpetual holy lamp at Athens was extinguished—one of three occasions Plutarch lists where such fires died and had to be rekindled from the sun.

characters.relation: Interrex who presided over Numa's election
characters.blurb: The Roman senator who happened to hold the interregnum at the moment the combined senate voted; he put the choice to a formal vote by which all declared Numa king—the procedural figure at Numa's accession.

characters.relation: Tragic poet with whom Aesculapius sojourned
characters.blurb: Athenian tragedian; the tradition that the god Aesculapius actually lodged with Sophocles during his lifetime (with proofs still visible in Plutarch's day) is cited as precedent for Numa's divine visitation by Egeria.

characters.relation: One of two Greeks immortalized in bronze at Rome
characters.blurb: Athenian statesman; Plutarch recalls hearing that when an oracle told the Romans to erect statues to the wisest and most valiant Greek, they chose Pythagoras for wisdom and Alcibiades for valor—a tradition linking Numa's Pythagorean city to Greek ideals.
